rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Response Without Objections, To Requests For Admission, Set One, Requests For Production Of Documents Set One, And For Interrogatories, Set One; And Request For Order Awarding Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 8, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC's Motion To Compel Further Response Without Objections, To Requests For Admission, Set One, Requests For Production Of Documents Set One, And For Interrogatories, Set One; And Request For Order Awarding Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Vestal, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion is GRANTED. Plaintiff shall serve verified further responses, without objections including claims of privilege and work product, to defendant's form interrogatory No. 17.1, requests for admissions Nos. 2 & 4 and inspection demand No. 1 within 21 days of the date of the court's order. Plaintiff and its counsel of record are further ordered to pay $4,125 in discovery sanctions to defendant by September 15, 2019 if it does not timely serve the further responses. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pvestal@nvlawllp.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DONALD NORMAN VS. PATRICK STRATEMAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Patrick Strateman And Jamie Strateman'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 8, 2019, Line 2, Defendants Patrick Strateman And Jamie Strateman's Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). (Continued from Part 1 of 2) Mr. Strateman's motion for summary adjudication is denied on the conversion, unjust enrichment, and accounting causes of action. The conversion and unjust enrichment causes of action can be based on the alleged improper salary draw. Defendant Jamie Strateman's motion for summary judgment is denied. As explained in the companion motion, defendants fail to show that the statute of limitations bars plaintiff's action. The motion for summary adjudication is denied on the aiding and abetting claims. Ms. Strateman fails to show that she did not aid and abet her son's breach of fiduciary duty and conversion. (See Defendants' UMF 359-371; 706-718.) In addition, plaintiff presents evidence that Ms. Strateman knew that her son was drawing a salary, failed to tell plaintiff, and represented to plaintiff that her son was doing everything in his power to preserve the LLC's value. (Norman Dec., Para. 21; J. Strateman Depo., 51:21-52:19; 119:11-120:3; 131:12-132:1; 135:16-137:1.) Ms. Strateman's motion for summary adjudication is granted on the unjust enrichment claim. Plaintiff did not oppose this request and there is no evidence that Ms. Strateman (as opposed to her son) was unjustly enriched. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (This is the end of the tentative ruling.) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DONALD NORMAN VS. PATRICK STRATEMAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 8, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANTS PATRICK STRATEMAN AND JAMIE STRATEMAN'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. (Part 1 of 2) Defendant Patrick Strateman's motion for summary judgment or alternatively summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. Defendants fail to show that the statute of limitations bars plaintiff's claims. The parties agree that Delaware law applies as this case involves the internal affairs of a Delaware LLC. In In re Verisign, Inc. (2007) 531 F.Supp.2d 1173, 1215, the court explains: Delaware applies a three-year statute of limitations to claims of tort, contract, and breach of fiduciary duty. [citation] The three-year limitation period is also applied to equitable claims by analogy. [citations] "The statute of limitations begins to run at the time that the cause of action accrues, which is generally when there has been a harmful act by a defendant. This is true even if the plaintiff is unaware of the cause of action or the harm." The parties further agree that the statute of limitations is measured from June 25, 2016, when plaintiff filed his federal complaint. Defendants fail to produce evidence showing that plaintiff's claims accrued by June of 2013. Defendants' evidence shows that by September of 2012, plaintiff had some doubts about Mr. Strateman and wondered whether he was acting in the best interests of the LLC. The deposition testimony that defendant relies upon is fairly vague, however, and does not show that plaintiff was aware that defendant took the LLC's bitcoins or caused other concrete damage to the LLC. (Defendants' UMF 1-17.) Plaintiff testified that during that time period, he did not believe that defendant would steal from the LLC. (Norman Depo., 248:18-22.) Defendants fail to maintain their summary judgment burden of production. Mr. Strateman's motion for summary adjudication on the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action is denied. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether he was the managing member of the LLC. (See Defendants' UMF 19, 20.) Mr. Strateman admits that by September 2012, he was the only employee of the LLC and he was singlehandedly operating it. (See Defendants' UMF 137, 139, 140.) There is also a disputed issue of fact whether he breached his duty to the LLC and caused damage. For example, there is a dispute whether Mr. Strateman froze out plaintiff and whether he was allowed to take a salary. (Strateman Dec., Paras. 7-8; Norman Dec., Paras. 3, 10, 18.) (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: VERONICA MCCLUSKEY VS. JEFF HENRY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Lift Stay Pursuant To 9 U.S.C.3 And C.C.P. 1281.4
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 8, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF VERONICA MCCLUSKEY'S Motion To Lift Stay Pursuant To 9 U.S.C.3 And C.C.P. 1281.4. Plaintiff Veronica McCluskey's motion to lift stay is denied. By order filed November 7, 2018, this Court granted defendants' motion to compel arbitration. Contrary to Plaintiff's contention, Defendants are not in "default" in the arbitration proceedings. Rather, the American Arbitration Association made a clerical error by misapplying defendants' timely fees and then, as a result, administratively closed the case. Once the AAA realized and acknowledged its mistake, it requested confirmation from plaintiff that she wants the case reopened. Plaintiff's counsel did not respond to that repeated request by the AAA. The Court will not allow Plaintiff to take advantage of the AAA's clerical error and her own lengthy delays in order to evade her contractual obligation to arbitrate her claims, if she wishes to pursue them. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: NEXTARTS, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. ROBERT R SCHMIDT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Relief Regarding This Courts Order Sustaining Defendants Demurrer To Complaint Without Leave To Amend
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 8, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF NEXTARTS, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION'S Motion For Relief Regarding This Courts Order Sustaining Defendants Demurrer To Complaint Without Leave To Amend. Plaintiff NextArts's motion for relief regarding this Court's order sustaining defendants' demurrer to complaint without leave to amend is granted on the conditions that plaintiff's counsel (1) file the first amended complaint within 5 days of the Court's order and (2) pay defendants $1,500 to reimburse them for the costs they incurred in filing the demurrer and attending the hearing. Plaintiff's counsel failed to file a timely opposition to defendants' demurrer, believing (incorrectly) that he could do so at any time prior to the hearing on the demurrer. As he acknowledges, Code of Civil Procedure section 472 was amended effective January 1, 2016, to require that an amended complaint be filed and served "no later than the date for filing an opposition to the demurrer." Plaintiff's counsel then failed to file the proposed first amended complaint, instead merely attaching it to plaintiff's "response" to defendant's demurrer; and he then failed timely to appear at the hearing, although he had notified the Court and opposing counsel that he intended to appear to contest the tentative ruling sustaining the demurrer without leave to amend. While the Court does not condone these multiple deficiencies, it exercises its discretion to relieve Plaintiff of the consequences of its counsel's failings in the interest of hearing the matter on its merits, particularly because Plaintiff's counsel acted diligently to set aside the order and Defendants have not shown that they will be prejudiced. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DENTAL FORCE, INC. ET AL VS. BEMDAD BEHNOOD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion (1) To Bifurcate The Cross-Complaint Of Defendant And Cross-Complaint Bamdad Benhood;(2) Set A Separate Trial Date As To The Cross-Complaint; (3) Have Discovery As To The Cross-Complaint Proceed On A Separate Track; And (4) Have The Trial Date As To The Cross-Complaint Be Deemed The Intial Trial Date As To That Pleading Only;
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 8, 2019, Line 6, CROSS-DEFENDANTS DENTAL FORCE, INC., EVANGELISTA DENTAL GROUP SAN FRANCISCO 450 SUTTER, AND RICHARD'S Motion (1) To Bifurcate The Cross-Complaint Of Defendant And Cross-Complaint Bamdad Benhood;(2) Set A Separate Trial Date As To The Cross-Complaint; (3) Have Discovery As To The Cross-Complaint Proceed On A Separate Track; And (4) Have The Trial Date As To The Cross-Complaint Be Deemed The Intial Trial Date As To That Pleading Only. The motion by plaintiffs and cross-defendants Dental Force, Inc. and Evangelista Dental Group San Francisco 450 Sutter, POC and cross-defendant Richard Evangelista (1) to bifurcate the cross-complaint; (2) set a separate trial date as to the cross-complaint; (3) have discovery as to the cross-complaint proceed on a separate track; and (4) have the trial date as to the cross-complaint be deemed the initial trial date as to that pleading only is denied. There are likely to be some common witnesses, and it would serve judicial economy for the entire case to be resolved in one trial, rather than two separate trials. If the moving parties need additional time to complete discovery or to move for summary judgment on the cross-complaint, they may seek to continue the trial date. This ruling is without prejudice to the parties requesting the trial judge to bifurcate the cross-complaint or certain issues for trial. (See Grappo v. Coventry Financial Corp. (1991) 235 Cal.App.3d 496, 504 ["trial courts have broad discretion to determine the order of proof in the interests of judicial economy"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant and cross-complainant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BENICIA MULLIKIN VS. KELLY DOMER PARTNERS, INC.A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 8, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT KELLY DOMER PARTNERS, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Kelly Domer Partners, Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiff Benicia Mullikin's complaint is off calendar for failure to comply with statutory meet and confer requirements. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 430.41.) Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41 requires that the demurring party, prior to filing the demurrer, "meet and confer in person or by telephone" with the party who filed the complaint with the purpose of attempting to "resolve the objections to be raised in the demurrer." (Id. Sec. 430.41(a).) The declaration filed by Defendant's counsel does not indicate that counsel met and conferred by phone or in person for purpose of resolving objections to be raised by demurrer. Plaintiff's counsel declares that no telephonic or in-person meet and confer occurred regarding the instant demurrer. (Kopczynski Decl. Para. 10.) Although Defendant's counsel sent three letters identifying potential defects in the complaint, he never mentioned his intention to file a demurrer on those grounds. Plaintiff's counsel indicates that when they spoke by phone on June 26, 2019 about discovery and a site inspection, Defendant's counsel did not mention that he was preparing a demurrer, which was filed nine days later. (Id. Para. 12.) Plaintiff's counsel learned of the demurrer three days after it was filed. (Id. Para. 16.) The parties are therefore ordered to meet and confer immediately. Defendant has until September 6, 2019 to respond to the complaint. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GLENN MAHLER ET AL VS. JUDICIAL COUNCIL OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 8, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANTS JUDICIAL COUNCIL OF CALIFORNIA AND TANI CANTIL-SAKAUYE'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Judicial Council of California and Chief Justice Tani G. Cantil-Sakauye's demurrer to plaintiffs' first amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend. Both causes of action in plaintiffs' first amended complaint are precluded by the doctrine of legislative immunity, which bars actions against judicial officers when they act in a legislative capacity. Because the Court previously set forth its detailed analysis of this ground in its July 10, 2019 order denying plaintiffs' motion for a preliminary injunction, and plaintiffs rest entirely on their prior briefing, the Court will not repeat that analysis here. As it is dispositive, the Court need not reach any of the other issues raised by defendants in their demurrer. However, plaintiffs' concessions in their opposition brief serve to further narrow the issues, in two respects. First, plaintiffs concede that they cannot recover damages for Chief Justice Cantil-Sakauye's discretionary acts in administering the Temporary Assigned Judges Program. (Gov. Code Sec. 820.2 ["a public employee is not liable for an injury resulting from [her] act or omission where the act or omission was the result of the exercise of discretion vested in [her], whether or not such discretion be abused"]; see Caldwell v. Montoya (1995) 10 Cal.4th 972 [holding that individual members of an elected school board are immune under Sec. 802.2 from a suit seeking damages against them personally for their votes to terminate the employment of the school district's superintendent, even when the complaint alleges race and age discrimination in violation of the Fair Employment and Housing Act].) As defendants correctly point out, that concession provides further support for the Court's conclusion that the complaint is barred by legislative immunity, because the statutory immunity "applies only to deliberate and considered policy decisions, in which a '[conscious] balancing [of] risks and advantages . . . took place.'" (Caldwell v. Montoya, 10 Cal.4th at 981, quoting Johnson v. State of California (1995) 69 Cal.2d 782, 795, fn. 8 [emphasis deleted].) Second, plaintiffs do not contest defendants' showing that their second cause of action, which is brought under article VI, section 6 of the California Constitution, fails to state a claim. That is so both (i) because there is no private right of action to enforce that provision and (ii) because the first amended complaint does not allege any facts constituting a violation of article VI, section 6. Further, plaintiffs previously admitted that the second cause of action does not present an independent ground for recovery. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the application was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
